title: Next-Token Prediction and Regret Minimization
abstract:
We consider the question of how to employ next-token prediction algorithms in adversarial online decision-making environments. Specifically, if we train a next-token prediction model on a distribution $\mathcal{D}$ over sequences of opponent actions, when is it the case that the induced online decision-making algorithm (by approximately best responding to the model's predictions) has low adversarial regret (i.e., when is $\mathcal{D}$ a \emph{low-regret distribution})?
  For unbounded context windows (where the prediction made by the model can depend on all the actions taken by the adversary thus far), we show that although not every distribution $\mathcal{D}$ is a low-regret distribution, every distribution $\mathcal{D}$ is exponentially close (in TV distance) to one low-regret distribution, and hence sublinear regret can always be achieved at negligible cost to the accuracy of the original next-token prediction model. In contrast to this, for bounded context windows (where the prediction made by the model can depend only on the past $w$ actions taken by the adversary, as may be the case in modern transformer architectures), we show that there are some distributions $\mathcal{D}$ of opponent play that are $Θ(1)$-far from any low-regret distribution $\mathcal{D'}$ (even when $w = Ω(T)$ and such distributions exist). Finally, we complement these results by showing that the unbounded context robustification procedure can be implemented by layers of a standard transformer architecture, and provide empirical evidence that transformer models can be efficiently trained to represent these new low-regret distributions.

sections.heading: 1Introduction
sections.text:
Large language models are trained to perform well at the task of next-token prediction: given some substring of text, estimate the conditional distribution of the next word/token. Increasingly, there is a focus on using these models to perform a far broader set of tasks, including making strategic decisions on our behalf (Chen et al. , 2021 ; Park et al. , 2025 ; Krishnamurthy et al. , 2024 ; Nie et al. , 2025 ) .

In many real applications, online decision-making is assisted with a next-token predictor. In algorithmic trading, next-token predictors are trained to predict market moves (e.g., Zhang et al. 2019 ; Kaggle 2018 ). The “tokens” here are not words, but the next element of a market sequence, e.g., the next price move or order-book event, conditioned on the recent history.
Decision makers are traders who decide policies with the assistance of the next-token predictor.
In supply-chain and inventory management, decision-making is similarly assisted with demand predictors.
Next-token predictors are trained for item-level demand trajectories across many related products and locations (e.g., Salinas et al. 2020 ).
The “tokens” here are the next elements of an operational sequence, e.g., the next-period demand realization, replenishment arrival.
Decision makers are inventory planners who choose replenishment policies, e.g., order quantities, transshipment decisions. The payoffs of the decision makers are jointly determined by their decisions and the predicted payoff-relevant state.

We formalize the problem as training such a next-token predictor to play a repeated game. Rock-paper-scissors is one simple example of a game.
Like in next-token prediction, the decision maker has to consider the historical actions taken in the game so far (a subsequence of tokens) and, from this, come up with a new mixed action to take (a distribution over next tokens). If the opponent’s actions are stochastically drawn by nature, then it makes sense that playing well in this game directly corresponds to how well our model can predict the next token. However, if we think of the tokens as the adversary’s actions, where things differ is in how these tokens are generated – instead of being stochastically sampled from a large data set, they are adversarially chosen by an opposing player who wants the model to fail. One basic property we might desire from these models in such settings is adversarial regret minimization . That is, regardless of what actions the adversary takes, our model does at least as well as if it always played the best fixed action in hindsight.

We would like a predictor to perform well when the states are stochastically drawn, and guarantees regret minimization when they are adversarially generated. This raises the question: can we achieve a best-of-both-worlds guarantee from a next-token predictor? Are regret minimization and next-token prediction compatible goals? When is it the case that training a next-token predictor on a dataset (e.g., of game transcripts) will produce a low-regret learning algorithm? Are there ways to automatically augment a data set with more data so the resulting models have less regret? What alternatives to next-token prediction are there when training these models?

sections.heading: 1.1Our Results
sections.text:
We study an online decision making setting where a decision maker needs to take actions in response to a changing state of nature (e.g. an adversary’s action in a game, a current stock price, etc.). The decision maker has access to a next-token prediction model trained on some distribution of state sequences, and would like use the predictions from this model to help them make utility optimizing decisions.

Importantly, they would like to achieve the best-of-both-worlds guarantee. This leads to the question of whether it is possible to robustify a decision-model: take a model ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} and produce a model ℳ \mathcal{M} that represents a similar distribution over sequences as ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} , while guaranteeing low regret against any adversary.

We prove the following results.

• First, we remark that there exist next-token prediction models such that if a decision maker approximately best responds to these predictions (e.g., via a quantal best response), they guarantee sublinear regret. In particular, quantal best responses to the Polya urn process closely simulate the classical Hedge learning algorithm ( Lemma 2.3 ) . • Second, we positively answer the question of robustification by showing that given any next-token prediction model ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} it is possible to produce a model ℳ \mathcal{M} such that i. quantal best responses to the predictions of ℳ \mathcal{M} lead to sublinear regret, and ii. the TV distance between the distributions represented by ℳ \mathcal{M} and ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} is arbitrarily small ( Theorem 3.1 ) . • We then shift our attention to prediction models with bounded context length (i.e., prediction models whose outputs can only depend on the previous L L tokens). In contrast to the previous result, we show that such models are in general impossible to robustify ( Theorem 4.1 ) . However, if the robustified model is allowed to use a larger context length L ′ L^{\prime} , it is possible to produce a robust model with O ​ ( 1 / L ′ − L ) O(1/\sqrt{L^{\prime}-L}) per-round regret ( Theorem 4.2 ) . • Finally, we address the question of whether it is actually possible to train robust models, with a focus on transformer models. We provide two pieces of evidence towards an affirmative answer to this question. First, we show that transformer models can effectively represent the robustified models of Theorem 3.1 with a mild increase in size ( Theorem 5.1 ) . Second, we provide experimental evidence that it is possible to train small transformers to represent robustified versions of simple distributions ( Section 5.2 ) .

sections.heading: 1.2Related Work
sections.text: Our study is at the intersection of decision-making in online learning as well as modern transformer architectures in deep learning.

sections.heading: 2.1Next-Token Prediction
sections.text:
The problem of next-token prediction can be formally stated as follows. We are given a distribution D ∈ Δ ​ ( Θ T ) D\in\Delta(\Theta^{T}) over sequences of T T tokens from an alphabet Θ \Theta . The goal is to learn a (next-token prediction) model ℳ \mathcal{M} that, given as input any prefix token sequence 𝜽 t − 1 = ( θ 1 , … , θ t − 1 ) \bm{\theta}^{t-1}=(\theta_{1},\dots,\theta_{t-1}) , outputs the conditional distribution of the next token given this prefix, which we denote by ℳ ​ ( 𝜽 t − 1 ) ∈ Δ ​ ( Θ ) \mathcal{M}(\bm{\theta}^{t-1})\in\Delta(\Theta) . We write ℳ ​ ( θ ∣ 𝜽 t − 1 ) \mathcal{M}(\theta\mid\bm{\theta}^{t-1}) to denote the probability of a specific token θ ∈ Θ \theta\in\Theta in the distribution ℳ ​ ( 𝜽 t − 1 ) \mathcal{M}(\bm{\theta}^{t-1}) .

By iterating the operation of next token prediction, any candidate solution ℳ \mathcal{M} to the next token prediction problem induces its own distribution D ​ ( ℳ ) D(\mathcal{M}) over sequences of T T tokens. In particular, we can define ℙ 𝜽 T ∼ D ​ ( ℳ ) [ 𝜽 T ] \displaystyle\operatorname*{\mathbb{P}}_{\bm{\theta}^{T}\sim D(\mathcal{M})}[\bm{\theta}^{T}] = \displaystyle= ℳ ​ ( θ 1 | ∅ ) ​ ℳ ​ ( θ 2 | θ 1 ) ​ ℳ ​ ( θ 3 | θ 1 , θ 2 ) ​ ⋯ ​ ℳ ​ ( θ T | 𝜽 T − 1 ) . \displaystyle\mathcal{M}(\theta_{1}|\emptyset)\mathcal{M}(\theta_{2}|\theta_{1})\mathcal{M}(\theta_{3}|\theta_{1},\theta_{2})\cdots\mathcal{M}(\theta_{T}|\bm{\theta}^{T-1}).

Conversely, every distribution D D corresponds 1 1 1 For mathematical convenience, we will assume that all distributions D D we consider have full support – that is, every sequence in Θ T \Theta^{T} appears with some positive (albeit possibly arbitrarily small) probability in the distribution. Under this assumption, this correspondence is bijective. to some model (in the sense that it is induced by a collection of conditional distribution functions ℳ ​ ( θ t | 𝜽 t − 1 ) \mathcal{M}(\theta_{t}|\bm{\theta}^{t-1}) ). We can therefore measure the quality of a solution ℳ \mathcal{M} to the next-token prediction problem via the TV distance d T ​ V ​ ( D , D ​ ( ℳ ) ) d_{TV}(D,D(\mathcal{M})) between the true distribution and the distribution induced by the model. Likewise, we can measure the similarity between two models ℳ \mathcal{M} and ℳ ′ \mathcal{M}^{\prime} via the TV distance of their respective distributions.

sections.heading: 2.2Adversarial Online Decision Making
sections.text:
The second problem we consider is that of (adversarial) online decision making . In this problem, a decision maker interacts with an adversary over the course of T T rounds. In each round t ∈ [ T ] t\in[T] of interaction, the learner takes an action (specifically, a mixed action π t ∈ Δ ​ ( A ) \pi_{t}\in\Delta(A) supported on some finite action set A A ) while, simultaneously, an adversary selects a state θ t ∈ Θ \theta_{t}\in\Theta . As a result of this interaction, the decision maker receives expected utility 𝔼 a t ∼ π t [ U ​ ( a , θ t ) ] \operatorname*{\mathbb{E}}_{a_{t}\sim\pi_{t}}[U(a,\theta_{t})] , where the utility function U : A × Θ → [ − 1 , 1 ] U:A\times\Theta\rightarrow[-1,1] is known to all parties and fixed over time (we extend U U linearly to mixed strategies of the decision maker by writing U ​ ( π , θ ) = 𝔼 a ∼ π [ U ​ ( a , θ ) ] U(\pi,\theta)=\operatorname*{\mathbb{E}}_{a\sim\pi}[U(a,\theta)] ). After this interaction, the state θ t \theta_{t} chosen by the adversary is revealed to the learner, who can then use this information in the selection of their subsequent actions.

The goal of the decision maker is to maximize their cumulative utility over all T T rounds. Of course, the extent to which they can do so depends on the adversarial choices of θ t \theta_{t} taken by the adversary (notably, unlike in the next-token prediction problem, the sequence of states 𝜽 T = ( θ 1 , θ 2 , … , θ T ) \bm{\theta}^{T}=(\theta_{1},\theta_{2},\dots,\theta_{T}) is not necessarily sampled from some distribution D D ). Despite this, one of the fundamental results in the theory of online learning shows that regardless of the actions taken by the adversary, it is possible for the decision maker to obtain sublinear regret : the gap between their cumulative utility and the cumulative utility of the best fixed action in hindsight. Formally, given a sequence of (mixed) actions 𝝅 = ( π 1 , … , π T ) \bm{\pi}=(\pi_{1},\dots,\pi_{T}) and states 𝜽 = ( θ 1 , … , θ T ) \bm{\theta}=(\theta_{1},\dots,\theta_{T}) , we define the external regret as ExtReg ​ ( 𝝅 , 𝜽 ) = max a ∗ ∈ A ⁡ 1 T ​ ∑ t [ U ​ ( a ∗ , θ t ) − U ​ ( π t , θ t ) ] . \textsc{ExtReg}(\bm{\pi},\bm{\theta})=\max_{a^{*}\in A}\frac{1}{T}\sum_{t}\left[U(a^{*},\theta_{t})-U(\pi_{t},\theta_{t})\right]. One algorithm that guarantees sublinear regret for the decision maker is the Hedge algorithm (Freund and Schapire, 1997 ) . The Hedge algorithm chooses π t \pi_{t} so that (for any a ∈ A a\in A ) π t ​ ( a ) ∝ exp ⁡ ( 1 T ​ ∑ s = 1 t − 1 U ​ ( a , θ s ) ) \pi_{t}(a)\propto\exp\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{T}}\sum_{s=1}^{t-1}U(a,\theta_{s})\right) . It can be shown that this guarantees that ExtReg ​ ( 𝝅 , 𝜽 ) = O ​ ( log ⁡ | A | ​ 1 / T ) \textsc{ExtReg}(\bm{\pi},\bm{\theta})=O(\log|A|\sqrt{1/T}) , regardless of the sequence of states chosen by the adversary.

sections.heading: 2.3Interplay between Next-Token Prediction and Regret Minimization
sections.text:
One natural way to apply a next-token prediction algorithm to the problem of online decision making is by using it to predict the sequence of adversary states. In particular, the decision maker can use an algorithm for next-token prediction to predict the next state, and then play the optimal action conditioned on this state. Formally, for any distribution μ ∈ Δ ​ ( Θ ) \mu\in\Delta(\Theta) over states, let BR ​ ( μ ) = argmax a ∈ A 𝔼 θ ∼ μ [ U ​ ( a , θ ) ] \text{BR}(\mu)=\operatorname*{argmax}_{a\in A}\operatorname*{\mathbb{E}}_{\theta\sim\mu}[U(a,\theta)] be the decision maker’s best response action to this distribution. In online decision making in stochastic settings (where the sequence of states 𝜽 \bm{\theta} is drawn from some distribution D D ), best responding to the predictions of an accurate model leads to zero external regret.

However, we would like stronger guarantees than this – ideally, we would like to construct an online decision making algorithm with adversarial regret guarantees (e.g., those obtained by Hedge). This leads to the question: does there exist a distribution D D where the online decision making algorithm constructed in Lemma 2.1 incurs o ​ ( 1 ) o(1) regret against any adversary? Unfortunately, the answer to this question is negative, as the following lemma demonstrates.

Ultimately, the negative result in Lemma 2.2 follows from the fact that the learning algorithms constructed by best responding to a sequence of next-token prediction are deterministic (in the sense of always playing pure actions in A A ).

We can attempt to sidestep this issue by introducing noise in the best response of the decision maker. One natural and well-studied way to do this is to replace the best response with a quantal best response 2 2 2 This response function is also known under many other names, including softmax response , Boltzmann exploration , and multinomial logit response . . Given a distribution μ ∈ Δ ​ ( Θ ) \mu\in\Delta(\Theta) over states and a parameter η > 0 \eta>0 , we define the quantal best response QBR ​ ( μ , η ) ∈ Δ ​ ( A ) \text{QBR}(\mu,\eta)\in\Delta(A) to be the mixed action that plays action a ∈ A a\in A with probability proportional to exp ⁡ ( 1 η ​ U ​ ( a , μ ) ) \exp(\frac{1}{\eta}\,U(a,\mu)) . Note that as η → 0 \eta\rightarrow 0 , this approaches the deterministic best response (and as η → ∞ \eta\rightarrow\infty , this approaches the uniform distribution over all actions).

We define the Polya urn model ℳ Polya \mathcal{M}_{\text{Polya}} to be the following next-token prediction model: for any t ∈ [ T ] t\in[T] , we let ℳ Polya ​ ( θ | 𝜽 ( t − 1 ) ) = 1 + ∑ s = 1 t − 1 𝕀 ​ [ θ s = θ ] | Θ | + ( t − 1 ) . \mathcal{M}_{\text{Polya}}(\theta|\bm{\theta}^{(t-1)})=\frac{1+\sum_{s=1}^{t-1}\mathbb{I}\left[\theta_{s}=\theta\right]}{|\Theta|+(t-1)}. (1)

Intuitively, the probability of seeing a specific token θ \theta at round t t is roughly equal to the empirical probability of observing θ \theta in the string so far. More accurately, it is exactly the fraction of tokens equal to θ \theta in the string Str ​ ( Θ ) + 𝜽 ( t − 1 ) \text{Str}(\Theta)+\bm{\theta}^{(t-1)} , where Str ​ ( Θ ) \text{Str}(\Theta) is an arbitrary concatenation of all the tokens in Θ \Theta (it is necessary to add this additional term so that equation 1 is well-defined for t = 1 t=1 , and so that the induced distribution D ​ ( ℳ Polya ) D(\mathcal{M}_{\text{Polya}}) has full support). The following lemma shows that quantal best responses to predictions of the Polya urn model guarantee adversarial low regret.

Motivated by Lemma 2.3 , we formalize the property of a low-regret model, requiring decision makers to achieve low regret when quantal responding to next-token predictions.

sections.heading: 3Robustification with Unbounded Context Length
sections.text:
Lemma 2.3 demonstrates that the Polya urn model is a low-regret model – following its recommendations (by quantally best responding to them) will result in adversarial low-regret guarantees for an online decision maker. While it is possible to construct other low-regret models similarly, not every model is low-regret. For example, the model ℳ \mathcal{M} for binary states ( Θ = { 0 , 1 } \Theta=\{0,1\} ) which always predicts the next bit to be 1 1 with probability 1 / 3 1/3 can be shown to incur Ω ​ ( 1 ) \Omega(1) regret against adversarial sequences of states (e.g., if the adversary selects the all-zero sequence of states θ t = 0 \theta_{t}=0 , this model will never predict the next state correctly).

This raises a natural question. Assume we have access to a next-token model ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} . Can we “robustify” our model and obtain a new model ℳ \mathcal{M} that is both low-regret and close to the original model ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} (in the sense that the distributions D 0 D_{0} and D D they induce are similar in TV distance)?

In this section, we answer this question affirmatively. In Algorithm 1 , we give a procedure for taking an arbitrary next-token prediction model ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} and transforming it into a low-regret next-token prediction model ℳ \mathcal{M} . The key idea is to only modify the behavior of the model on prefixes 𝜽 t \bm{\theta}^{t} where the model has already incurred high regret (by arguments similar to those in Lemma 2.1 , this should happen with low probability if the sequence of states truly is sampled from D ​ ( ℳ 0 ) D(\mathcal{M}_{0}) ). On such high-regret prefixes, we instead draw the prediction of the model from a Polya urn model, guaranteeing low-regret on the remainder of the time horizon.

sections.heading: 4Robustification with a Bounded Context Length
sections.text:
In the previous section, we concerned ourselves with next-token prediction models whose prediction of the state θ t \theta_{t} at time t t could depend on all previous states 𝜽 t − 1 \bm{\theta}^{t-1} . In practice, most next-token prediction models (e.g. those based on transformer architectures) are autoregressive models restricted by a context length L L . That is to say, the model’s prediction ℳ ​ ( θ t | 𝜽 t − 1 ) \mathcal{M}(\theta_{t}|\bm{\theta}^{t-1}) is a round-independent function of the previous L L tokens 𝜽 ( t − L ) : ( t − 1 ) = ( θ t − L , θ t − L + 1 , … , θ t − 1 ) \bm{\theta}^{(t-L):(t-1)}=(\theta_{t-L},\theta_{t-L+1},\dots,\theta_{t-1}) . When t ≤ L t\leq L , then ℳ ​ ( θ t | 𝜽 t − 1 ) \mathcal{M}(\theta_{t}|\bm{\theta}^{t-1}) can be an arbitrary function of the past tokens (as in the unbounded context case). We will refer to such models as L L -bounded models for short.

As before, every bounded context model ℳ \mathcal{M} induces a distribution D ​ ( ℳ ) D(\mathcal{M}) over state sequences of length T T , and as before, we will measure the similarity of two models by the TV distance of their induced distributions.

We still would like to use these models to aid in adversarial online decision making 3 3 3 For technical reasons, in this section we will restrict ourselves to binary action settings ( | A | = 2 |A|=2 ). This has the consequence that the quantal best response function QBR ​ ( ⋅ , 1 / L ) \text{QBR}(\cdot,1/\sqrt{L}) has a convex image, which will be important for implementing some of the algorithms for online learning with bounded recall (e.g., see the second-to-last line of Algorithm 2 ). . Of course, the limited context window of these models constrains what regret guarantees are possible. The setting of online learning with bounded recall studies online decision making instances where the action at round t t must be a function of the previous L L losses (i.e., states). It can be shown (Schneider and Vodrahalli, 2024 ) that in this setting, there are simple modifications of Hedge that guarantee at most O ​ ( L − 1 / 2 ) O(L^{-1/2}) regret against any adversary, and that this regret bound is tight (intuitively, this regret bound is achievable by restarting Hedge every L L rounds).

As in the unbounded context setting, we can use an L L -bounded model ℳ \mathcal{M} to solve online learning with L L -bounded recall by playing quantal best responses to the predictions of ℳ \mathcal{M} . In particular, we can show (in analogy to Lemma 2.3 ) that there exist L L -bounded models ℳ \mathcal{M} where if the decision maker plays π t = QBR ​ ( ℳ ​ ( 𝜽 t − 1 ) , 1 / L ) \pi_{t}=\text{QBR}(\mathcal{M}(\bm{\theta}^{t-1}),1/\sqrt{L}) , the decision maker guarantees O ​ ( 1 / L ) O(1/\sqrt{L}) regret for themselves.

We are then faced with the same question as in the previous section: if we start with an existing L L -bounded next-token prediction model ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} , can we robustify it into a model ℳ \mathcal{M} that is similar to ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} but also obtains optimal worst-case regret guarantees against an adversary?

sections.heading: 4.1Impossibility with the Same Context Length
sections.text:
We begin by demonstrating that, unlike in the unbounded context setting, robustification of bounded context models is in general impossible, even in very simple online decision-making settings (e.g. the adversarial online prediction problem with binary states).

Intuitively, this is because there can exist different L L -bounded models ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} and ℳ 1 \mathcal{M}_{1} that induce very different distributions D ​ ( ℳ 0 ) D(\mathcal{M}_{0}) and D ​ ( ℳ 1 ) D(\mathcal{M}_{1}) over sequences of length T T (in particular, almost never agreeing about the next token), but that share the same distribution of substrings of length L L . In particular, an L L -bounded model that can only ever see substrings of length L L will have trouble distinguishing whether the state sequence is being generated by ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} or ℳ 1 \mathcal{M}_{1} . If the goal is to robustify ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} , ℳ \mathcal{M} then has the impossible tradeoff between playing predictions close to that of ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} (guaranteeing low TV distance, but possibly incurring high regret with respect to sequences drawn from ℳ 1 \mathcal{M}_{1} ) or playing predictions that guarantee low regret for ℳ 1 \mathcal{M}_{1} (which cause a large TV distance with respect to ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} ).

sections.heading: 4.2Robustification with a Longer Context Length
sections.text:
In the previous section, we showed that there is no way to robustify an existing L L -bounded model ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} to a low-regret L L -bounded model ℳ \mathcal{M} (while implementing approximately the same distribution). In this section, we show that if we allow the robustified model to have a slightly larger context window L ′ L^{\prime} , we can effectively perform this robustification. Said another way, this fact implies that it is possible to learn a model that will length-generalize from the distribution of a sufficiently short sequence while maintaining no-regret guarantees in the bounded context setting (a more realistic setting for transformer-based models).

We do this by adapting the “AverageRestartHedge” algorithm of Schneider and Vodrahalli ( 2024 ) , which achieves O ​ ( 1 m ) O\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{m}}\right) external regret in adversarial online learning settings with m m -bounded recall. At a high level, this algorithm is configured with some non-constrained low-regret sub-algorithm (canonically, Hedge) as a subroutine. It then outputs the average prediction of this sub-algorithm on a uniformly randomly chosen suffix of the previous L L losses.

We will run a variant of this algorithm with Algorithm 1 in place of Hedge. Specifically, given an expanded context of length L ′ L^{\prime} , we use L L out of L ′ L^{\prime} tokens are used for next-token prediction under the original distribution D 0 D_{0} . The remaining Δ = L ′ − L \Delta=L^{\prime}-L tokens can then be viewed as the actual context length given to the online algorithm in Schneider and Vodrahalli ( 2024 ) . Our Algorithm 2 calls Algorithm 1 as a subroutine, which achieves an external regret of O ~ ​ ( 1 Δ ) \tilde{O}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{\Delta}}\right) (as implied by Theorem 4.1 , it is impossible to get non-trival guarantees when Δ = 0 \Delta=0 ).

sections.heading: 5Training Low-Regret Transformer Models
sections.text:
On one hand, Theorem 3.1 demonstrates that it is information theoretically possible to robustify any next-token prediction model ℳ \mathcal{M} with negligible changes to the underlying distribution. At the same time, this raises questions about whether we can actually train low-regret models (after all, if d TV ​ ( D , D 0 ) d_{\text{TV}}(D,D_{0}) is exponentially small, no training procedure can efficiently distinguish between samples drawn from D D and samples drawn from D 0 D_{0}

In this section we investigate this question for the special case of transformer models , providing evidence that it is possible to directly robustify low-regret transformer models. In Section 5.1 , we show it is possible to implement the operations of Algorithm 1 in the logic of a standard transformer model (i.e., if ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} can be represented by a small transformer, so can ℳ \mathcal{M} ). In Section 5.2 , we provide experimental evidence showing that a simple masking procedure allows us to practically train low-regret transformer models.

sections.heading: 5.1Representing Robustified Models
sections.text:
In this section, we show that the representational limitations of transformers pose no obstacle to robustification.
To that end, we construct a transformer that robustly predicts future states by adding a constant number of layers to a transformer that solves next-token prediction.

We state the theorem rigorously and present its proof in Section 7 .
At a high-level, the argument relies on constructing four layers that use the outputs of ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} to simulate Algorithm 1 .
Self-attention plays an essential role in the construction. Identifying the distribution induced by the Polya Urn strategy and calculating the two regret quantities involve computing aggregations over sequences of tokens, which are naturally simulated with self-attention layers.
Our construction reflects a realistic class of transformers by maintaining tight bounds on embedding dimension and depth and employing multi-layer perceptrons that can be compactly represented as shallow ReLU networks.

sections.heading: 5.2Empirically Robustifying Simple Transformers
sections.text:
In the previous sections, we demonstrated the existence of a procedure for learning specialized low-regret online learning algorithms by carefully perturbing the original statistical training data. In this section, we also demonstrate that in simple settings, it is also practically efficient to train small transformers with this algorithm, suggesting that robustification procedures may be practically plausible for modifying LLM behavior for decision-making while retaining good statistical performance.

We consider the special case of a decision problem to match the state. Both the action space and the state space are binary A = Θ = { 0 , 1 } A=\Theta=\{0,1\} , and the utility function U ​ ( a , θ ) = 𝕀 ​ [ a = θ ] U(a,\theta)=\mathbb{I}\left[a=\theta\right] . We conduct experiments with the minimal decoder-only transformer, NanoDO (Liu et al. , 2024 ) . The transformer predicts a binary sequence. We adopt the default parameters of NanoDO, with a context length of T = 1024 T=1024 , 256 256 embedding dimensions, 4 4 attention heads, 3 3 transformer block layers, and 1024 1024 inner dimensions.

We train the transformer on three datasets with a batch size of 128 128 . The three training processes all converge and stop after 500 500 steps.

sections.heading: 6.5Proof ofTheorem4.1
sections.text:
To formally construct these two models ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} and ℳ 1 \mathcal{M}_{1} , we will make use of the theory of de Bruijn sequences . The de Bruijn graph G ^ σ , k \hat{G}_{\sigma,k} of order k k on an alphabet Θ = { s 1 , … , s | Θ | } \Theta=\{s_{1},\dots,s_{|\Theta|}\} of size | Θ | |\Theta| is a directed graph whose vertices represent all distinct sequences of length k − 1 k-1 . For every sequence s i 1 ​ s i 2 ​ … ​ s i k s_{i_{1}}s_{i_{2}}\dots s_{i_{k}} of length k k , there is a directed edge from the vertex s i 1 ​ s i 2 ​ … ​ s i k − 1 s_{i_{1}}s_{i_{2}}\dots s_{i_{k-1}} to the vertex s i 2 ​ s i 3 ​ … ​ s i k s_{i_{2}}s_{i_{3}}\dots s_{i_{k}} . A de Bruijn sequence of order k k is a cyclic sequence of characters in Θ \Theta where each of the possible | Θ | k |\Theta|^{k} substrings of length k k appears exactly once. Note that a de Bruijn sequence of order k k corresponds to a (loop-removed) Eulerian cycle in a de Bruijn graph of order k k (which in turn must exist since every node in G ^ σ , k \hat{G}_{\sigma,k} has equal indegree and outdegree | Θ | |\Theta| ).

Given a fixed de Bruijn sequence of order L L and over a binary alphabet Θ = { 0 , 1 } \Theta=\{0,1\} , we can use it to construct two nearly deterministic L L -bounded models ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} and ℳ 1 \mathcal{M}_{1} . ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} and ℳ 1 \mathcal{M}_{1} induce the same uniform marginal distribution over length L L substrings, but the next-token predictions are different. We construct in the following way: we define ℳ 0 ​ ( 𝜽 L ) \mathcal{M}_{0}(\bm{\theta}^{L}) specified by the deterministic next-token of the de Bruijn sequence, and ℳ 1 ​ ( 𝜽 L ) = 1 − ℳ 0 ​ ( 𝜽 L ) \mathcal{M}_{1}(\bm{\theta}^{L})=1-\mathcal{M}_{0}(\bm{\theta}^{L}) as the deterministic opposite of ℳ 1 \mathcal{M}_{1} . The first L L tokens in the Markov process are seeded uniformly so that both processes remain in the same stationary distribution: the marginal distribution over any t > L t>L substring is uniform. Thus, any context length L L model ℳ \mathcal{M} will not be able to distinguish ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} from ℳ 1 \mathcal{M}_{1} . Such a model ℳ \mathcal{M} makes predictions very differently from the deterministic next-token of either ℳ 0 \mathcal{M}_{0} or ℳ 1 \mathcal{M}_{1} , leading to Theorem 4.1 .

sections.heading: 7.1Transformer Preliminaries
sections.text:
We introduce a formal model of a transformer, drawing heavily from Sanford et al. [ 2024 ] .

For a sequence of
queries, keys, and values Q , K , V ∈ ℝ T × m Q,K,V\in\mathbb{R}^{T\times m} ,
an autoregressive self-attention head of embedding dimension m m with softmax attention is defined by f ​ ( Q , K , V ) = softmax ​ ( Q ​ K 𝖳 ) ​ V , f(Q,K,V)=\mathrm{softmax}(QK^{\mathsf{T}})V, where the softmax operator softmax ​ ( v ) = 1 ∑ i = 1 T exp ⁡ ( v i ) ​ ( exp ⁡ ( v 1 ) , … , exp ⁡ ( v T ) ) \mathrm{softmax}(v)=\frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{T}\exp(v_{i})}(\exp(v_{1}),\dots,\exp(v_{T})) is applied row-wise, and mask M ∈ ℝ T × T M\in\mathbb{R}^{T\times T} satisfies M i , j = { 0 if ​ i ≥ j , − ∞ otherwise. M_{i,j}=\begin{cases}0&\text{if}\ i\geq j,\\
-\infty&\text{otherwise.}\end{cases}

Multi-headed attention concatenates the outputs for multiple attention heads.
For some sequential input X ∈ ℝ T × m X\in\mathbb{R}^{T\times m} , an H H -headed attention unit computes H H queries, keys, and values of embedding dimension m H \frac{m}{H} as Q h = X ​ W Q h , K h = X ​ W K k , V h = X ​ W V h , Q^{h}=XW_{Q}^{h},\ K^{h}=XW_{K}^{k},\ V^{h}=XW_{V}^{h}, for projections W Q h , W K h , W V h ∈ ℝ m × m / H W_{Q}^{h},W_{K}^{h},W_{V}^{h}\in\mathbb{R}^{m\times m/H} , for every h ∈ [ H ] h\in[H] .
The output of the resulting H H -headed attention layer is the following: X ↦ [ f ​ ( Q 1 , K 1 , V 1 ) ​ … ​ f ​ ( Q H , K H , V H ) ] , X\mapsto[f(Q^{1},K^{1},V^{1})\dots f(Q^{H},K^{H},V^{H})], for parameters ( W Q h , W K h , W V h ) h ∈ [ H ] (W_{Q}^{h},W_{K}^{h},W_{V}^{h})_{h\in[H]} .

We define a transformer of depth L L as a function of the form g = ϕ L ∘ f L ∘ ⋯ ∘ ϕ 1 ∘ f 1 ∘ ϕ 0 , g=\phi_{L}\circ f_{L}\circ\dots\circ\phi_{1}\circ f_{1}\circ\phi_{0}, where f 1 , … , f L f_{1},\dots,f_{L} are multi-headed attention layers of embedding dimension m m , and ϕ 1 , … , ϕ L − 1 : ℝ m → ℝ m \phi_{1},\dots,\phi_{L-1}:\mathbb{R}^{m}\to\mathbb{R}^{m} are multi-layer perceptrons applied element-wise, i.e. ϕ ℓ ​ ( X ) = ( ϕ ℓ ​ ( X 1 ) , … ​ ϕ ℓ ​ ( X T ) ) , \phi_{\ell}(X)=(\phi_{\ell}(X_{1}),\dots\phi_{\ell}(X_{T})), ϕ 0 : Σ → ℝ m \phi_{0}:\Sigma\to\mathbb{R}^{m} is an embedding layer from some alphabet Σ \Sigma , and ϕ L : ℝ m → ℝ d out \phi_{L}:\mathbb{R}^{m}\to\mathbb{R}^{d_{\mathrm{out}}} is an output MLP layer.
In the subsequent proof, we argue informally that our MLP units can be efficiently constructed as a shallow ReLU network with bounded width (typically, logarithmic in sequence length T T ) and bit-precision ( log ⁡ ( T ) \log(T) as well).

We assume that the alphabet Σ \Sigma encodes a positional encoding. That is, in the proof of Theorem 5.1 , we let Σ = Θ × [ T ] \Sigma=\Theta\times[T] and encode the input sequence θ T ∈ Θ T \theta^{T}\in\Theta^{T} as ( ( θ 1 , 1 ) , … , ( θ T , T ) ) ((\theta_{1},1),\dots,(\theta_{T},T)) . We assume that there exists a constant “beginning-of-sequence token” X BOS X_{\texttt{BOS}} that produces constant key and value vectors and can be attended to.

sections.heading: 7.2Proof ofTheorem5.1
sections.text:
We restate Theorem 5.1 precisely.

Before proving Theorem 7.1 , we observe that there are two senses in which the transformer construction is approximate: • The Regret s \textsc{Regret}_{s} condition makes no guarantees within an additive interval of width 2 ​ δ 2\delta . • The transformer guarantee does not account for the previous | A | |A| states in the outcomes.

Both issues are insignificant in the regime where T T is large, and Theorem 3.1 could be adapted in a straightforward manner to accommodate these changed conditions.

sections.heading: 8Generalization to Unknown Decision Problem
sections.text: We show that our method applies to the case where the decision problem is unknown and the state space is binary. This appendix defines a switching procedure that (i) discretizes the class of proper scoring rules using a finite basis of V-shaped proper scores, and (ii) switches to a PolyaUrn predictor whenever the primary predictor exhibits large external regret for some discretized V-shaped score. For generalization to a multi-dimensional case, similar results usually require a tractable cover of the set of multi-dimensional proper scoring rules. Kleinberg et al. [ 2023 ] has shown that the same additive decomposition in the binary case does not generalize to the case where the state space | Θ | ≥ 4 |\Theta|\geq 4 .

sections.heading: 8.1Setup and notation
sections.text:
We consider a binary outcome sequence θ 1 , θ 2 , ⋯ ∈ { 0 , 1 } \theta_{1},\theta_{2},\dots\in\{0,1\} . A (randomized) predictor outputs probabilities ℳ ​ ( θ t ∣ 𝜽 t − 1 ) ∈ [ 0 , 1 ] \mathcal{M}(\theta_{t}\mid\bm{\theta}^{t-1})\in[0,1] before observing θ t \theta_{t} .
For notational convenience in this appendix, we write p t := ℳ ​ ( θ t ∣ 𝜽 t − 1 ) p_{t}:=\mathcal{M}(\theta_{t}\mid\bm{\theta}^{t-1}) .

We define proper scoring rules, which will later be shown to correspond to the best-responding payoff of a decision problem.

sections.heading: 8.2V-shaped proper losses and discretization
sections.text: The space of proper scoring rules for binary states can be covered by a family of V-shaped scoring rules [Li et al. , 2022 , Kleinberg et al. , 2023 ] .

sections.heading: 8.3Switching rule and algorithm
sections.text:
Let p t 0 p_{t}^{0} be the primary predictor’s forecast at time t t .
We define a one-way switching procedure that outputs either the primary forecasts or the Polya Urn forecasts.

sections.heading: 8.4Technical Lemmas for V-shaped Decomposition
sections.text:
The analysis needs (i) a cover (approximation) of an arbitrary proper scoring rule by mixtures of V-shaped scoring rules and (ii) uniform concentration over the discretized family. We adopt the same technical lemmas from Kleinberg et al. 2023 , Hu and Wu 2024 .

By a uniform concentration bound, if the predictions are in-distribution, the regret is low with high probability.

figures.caption: Figure 1 : The regret of three transformers over 8 8 ground truth distributions. The three transformers are 1) ROBUST_BERNOULLI , robustified BERNOULLI , 2) POLYAURN , and 3) BERNOULLI . The eight ground truth distributions are: a) half Ber ( 2 / 3 ) (2/3) and then half Ber ( 1 / 3 ) (1/3) ; b) half Ber ( 1 / 3 ) (1/3) and half Ber ( 2 / 3 ) (2/3) , the same distribution that ROBUST_BERNOULLI and BERNOULLI were trained on; c) Ber ( 1 / 3 ) (1/3) ; d) Ber ( 2 / 3 ) (2/3) ; and four periodically changing distributions on the second row. The plot shows (very narrow) confidence intervals in light color.
